data.title: Malaysia arrests 2 over video of man in headscarf drinking alcohol
data.description:
A local man and a woman have been detained in connection with a video showing an individual wearing a headscarf while consuming alcohol in Malaysia.
Bukit Aman Criminal Investigation Department (CID) director Commissioner M Kumar said the duo, aged 22 and 24, were arrested at about 4.30pm on Wednesday in Subang Jaya, Selangor state, to assist in investigations.
“Initial investigations found that the individual seen wearing a headscarf and consuming alcohol in the video was a man,” he said in a statement on Wednesday.
Investigations are being conducted by the Special Investigation Unit under the CID Prosecution/Legal Division, he added.
“The case is being investigated under Section 298A of the Penal Code, which relates to acts that may cause disharmony, disunity or hostility on religious grounds, and Section 233 of the Communications and Multimedia Act 1998 for the improper use of network facilities or services,” Kumar said.
Under Section 298A of the Penal Code, an offence that creates religious disharmony or threatens unity carries a jail term of between two and five years upon conviction.
Meanwhile, Section 233 of the Communications and Multimedia Act provides for a fine of up to 500,000 ringgit (US$123,526) or imprisonment of up to two years, or both, if found guilty.
Kumar stressed that firm and uncompromising action would be taken against any individual who deliberately disregards religious sensitivities and creates a non-harmonious environment.
“We urge the public to be responsible and prudent social media users, and to refrain from making statements or sharing content that could undermine harmony and unity, particularly issues involving religion, race and royalty,” he said.
This story was first published by The Star

data.title: Year in women’s sport: England’s rugby, football wins, WNBA grows, India’s cricket victory
data.description:
A deafening roar at Twickenham capped a 2025 Women’s Rugby World Cup that smashed attendance records, while India’s first Women’s Cricket World Cup triumph sparked nationwide celebrations.
A’Ja Wilson delivered a jaw-dropping Women’s National Basketball Association (WNBA) season to redefine what dominance looks like and Sydney McLaughlin-Levrone raced to a sensational world title at a new distance in ‍the 400m.
While the year was full of lasting images of women in sport that reshaped their visibility, it was also marked by new franchises, bigger television audiences and greater advertising impact, sending women’s sport into 2026 on a wave of momentum.
Red Roses redefine rugby’s potential
England hosted and won a Rugby World Cup that shattered records and redefined the sport’s potential.
The more than 440,000 tickets sold was triple the number at the 2021 edition and the crescendo was at Twickenham where 81,885 fans – the biggest ⁠crowd to watch a women’s rugby match – witnessed England’s Red Roses triumph 33–13 over Canada.
Sally Horrox, World Rugby’s chief of Women’s Rugby, described the spectacle as “something girls’ dreams are made of”.
More than half the tournament spectators were female and a huge 50 per cent attended their first-ever women’s rugby match.
The event generated a peak TV audience of 5.8 million in the UK. While the US did not get out of the pool stage, the spotlight saw American Ilona Maher further cement herself as the world’s most followed rugby player – male or female – with more than nine million followers across Instagram and TikTok.

Record-breaking year for WNBA
The WNBA soared once more, bolstered by expansion to 13 teams and the electrifying play of Wilson leading the Las Vegas Aces to their third title in four seasons.
The season was marked by record-breaking viewership and attendance, increases in merchandise sales and substantial ‍new media rights deals.
After welcoming the Golden State Valkyries this year, the league will expand to 15 teams in 2026 with new franchises in Toronto and Portland. Another three franchises in Cleveland, Detroit and Philadelphia will bring the total to 18 by 2030.
Wilson became the first four-time WNBA MVP and won her third defensive player ‌of the year award. She also earned Finals MVP and recently graced the cover of Time magazine, prompting Nike to launch an ad that read: “Only a magazine can cover her.”

India’s cricketers shine bright
India’s maiden victory at ‍the Women’s Cricket World Cup turned the streets of Mumbai into a carnival as fireworks lit up the night sky, drivers honked their horns and crowds danced.
Similar celebrations broke out across the country as people gathered around TVs in tea stalls and homes in what felt like a cultural milestone and saw the brand value of India’s top women cricketers go through the roof.
Euros reach new peaks
In football, the European Championship in Switzerland reached new peaks, eclipsing ticket sales for the 2022 tournament with two games still to be played.
Switzerland’s dream run to their first quarter-final ignited new interest in the women’s game in the Alpine nation better known for producing top skiers and tennis players.
“We lost the game, but I think we gained a lot,” coach Pia Sundhage said after the hosts lost to Spain. “When people shouted my name [after the game], I ‌feel they were shouting for women’s football.”

England beat Spain 3-1 on penalties in the final.
European champions Arsenal shattered the women’s transfer record by signing Canadian forward Olivia Smith from Liverpool for £1 million (US$1.34 million), making her the first female player to break the seven-figure barrier.
McLaughlin-Levrone’s flat-out feat
In athletics, Sydney McLaughlin-Levrone starred at the World Championships in breathtaking fashion, seamlessly switching from the 400m hurdles to win the flat 400m in the fastest ‌time for ⁠40 years.
She clocked a stunning 47.78 seconds, second fastest in history behind East Germany’s Marita Koch, which was set during that nation’s state-sponsored doping era.

Women’s sport continues to grow
The year also saw the expansion of women’s sports, with the launch of several new leagues.
The Unrivaled 3x3 women’s basketball league was ‌co-founded by Napheesa Collier and Breanna Stewart as a domestic ‌option for players to remain and play in the US during the WNBA off-season. Rose BC, led by Chelsea Gray, won the inaugural title.
Despite being a fledgling league, Unrivaled paid players an average salary of US$220,000, compared with the average of US$120,000 the WNBA pays players per season.
Among its investors are tennis great Serena Williams, NBA players Trae Young, Franz Wagner and Moritz Wagner, and football’s Alex Morgan. Warner Bros/Discovery, which holds exclusive media rights, recently increased their stake in the league.
Other new leagues that debuted in 2025 were the Women’s Professional Baseball League, the first US pro women’s league in the sport since 1954; League One Volleyball; and the Northern Super League, Canada’s first professional women’s football league.

data.title: Hong Kong’s new legislators must strengthen child protection laws
data.description:
The Mandatory Reporting of Child Abuse Ordinance becomes operational in Hong Kong on January 20, and will enhance child protections. It contains a mechanism for the early and effective detection of child abuse cases. It requires particular professionals to report if, during their work, they come to suspect a child has been suffering serious harm, or is at real risk of it.
The acts considered serious harm include inflicting physical injury, forcing or enticing a child to participate in a sexual act, and intimidating, terrifying or denigrating the child in a severe or repeated way that impairs the child’s mental health. If a child’s basic needs are repeatedly neglected, causing its health or development to be endangered, this could also constitute causing serious harm.
However, progress notwithstanding, nobody should think “job done”. Unfinished business aplenty remains, and the government will hopefully take the initiative with a comprehensive package of proposals to protect the most vulnerable. Once it does so, the incoming Legislative Council must facilitate the legislation, and in the meantime, it should hold the government’s feet to the fire.
In 2021, for example, the Law Reform Commission proposed a new offence of “failure to protect a child or vulnerable person where the child’s or vulnerable person’s death or serious harm results from an unlawful act or neglect”. It would facilitate the prosecution of people who, for example, fail to protect a child in danger and for whom they are responsible.
A classic instance was five-year-old Yeung Chi-wai, born with Down’s syndrome, who died in horrifying circumstances after swallowing the drug methamphetamine hydrochloride that his carers had left lying around at home.
The Law Reform Commission proposal derived from a British law that, given its urgency, was fast-tracked through parliament in 2004. After four years, it must be hoped that this much-needed reform will also receive an expedited passage through the Legislative Council next year. After all, delays can – and do – cost lives.




There is, moreover, a pressing need for an updated child cruelty law, clarifying that offending conduct is prosecutable regardless of whether the child’s suffering or injury is physical or psychological in nature. As things stand, prosecutors have little difficulty in bringing charges when a victim has been physically harmed, backed by proof of wounds or bruises. But emotional damage is a different story, and the law needs tightening.
In 2023, moreover, the Court of Appeal, when considering the sentence imposed on an adult offender for “extreme and callous cruelty” towards two children, called for a strengthening of the penalties, given that the maximum sentence for child cruelty is 10 years’ imprisonment. It said the facts showed “yet again the need for a substantial increase in the maximum sentences for this offence”. However, its call has yet to be heeded, and further such cases could arise at any time.
And, as recommended by the Law Reform Commission in 2019, the sexual grooming of children on the internet must be criminalised, along with sexual messaging. In March, the Hong Kong police, having surveyed 2,000 students (from Form One to Form Four), reported that 63.5 per cent had faced sexual advances online. This reflects developments elsewhere, with Save the Children and Western Sydney University having revealed last year that over six in 10 children globally interacted with “unknown others”.
Also, given the effect exposure to pornography can have on young minds, internet service providers should be made to understand there will be criminal consequences if children under 18 are allowed access to adult websites. A 2021 survey of 989 people revealed that 62 per cent began looking at pornography when aged 10 to 15, with 15 per cent having encountered it while under nine. If children are to enjoy a healthy transition to adulthood, adult site operators must be subject to a duty of care.




As the statistics show, child abuse cases are rising, and alarm bells are ringing. Hong Kong reported 1,504 cases last year, up from 1,457 in 2023 and 1,439 in 2022. Shocking as they are, these figures are only the tip of the iceberg. For example, support group Talk Hong Kong estimates that although 16 per cent of children may experience sexual abuse (affecting one in five girls and one in nine boys), up to 96 per cent of cases never reach the authorities.
This was because stigma, fear, social taboos and cultural barriers prevent children and families from coming forward, and the same is equally true of other types of child abuse. But everybody needs to understand that such conduct is intolerable and that culprits will face the full force of the law.
Much has been said about the workload awaiting Hong Kong’s eighth term of Legco, and it will undoubtedly have its work cut out for it. But it must also find the time to enact the child protection laws that are urgently required and long overdue.

data.title: Golden era: prices surge as rally heads towards US$5,000 in 2026, analysts forecast
data.description:
Gold has hit multiple records in 2025, but analysts believe the rally is far from over, with some forecasting the yellow metal could climb to US$5,000 per ounce amid geopolitical tensions and a buying spree by central banks.
Spot gold broke through the US$4,500-per-ounce mark for the first time, reaching a record US$4,510 on Christmas Eve on Wednesday, which was 72 per cent higher than the end of last year, when it stood at US$2,624.
This was the biggest annual jump for the precious metal, exceeding the 70 per cent rise in 1979, according to Brian Fung, CEO of the Hong Kong Gold Exchange. The increase followed a 26 per cent surge in 2024.
Local prices rose in tandem, with gold in Hong Kong hitting a record HK$41,855 (US$5,382) per tael (37.51 grams) on Monday, according to the exchange.
Fung expected the rally to continue in 2026, with prices potentially hitting US$5,000 per ounce.
“The gold rally in 2025 was driven by expectations of interest-rate cuts, geopolitical tensions, and tariffs introduced by US President Donald Trump,” Fung told the Post. “Individual investors and central banks wanted to diversify away from US dollar assets, and gold became a safe-haven alternative.”

He said all the factors supporting gold showed “no sign of disappearing any time soon, which is why prices are set to go up further in 2026”.
Goldman Sachs lifted its gold price forecast for December 2026 to US$4,900 per ounce, with analyst Lina Thomas citing strong structural demand from central banks and easing by the US Federal Reserve.
Morgan Stanley predicted US$4,500 per ounce by mid-2026, while Bank of America and JPMorgan both expected prices to surpass US$5,000 per ounce by the end of 2026.
Fung said central bank buying remained a major driver of the rally. “Central banks traditionally invest in US Treasuries and other US dollar assets,” Fung said. “But amid rate cuts and geopolitical tensions, they want to decrease their holdings in US dollar and gold has become a natural choice.”
In October, China reduced its US Treasury holding to its lowest level in 17 years, falling to US$688.7 billion from US$700.5 billion in September, according to US Treasury Department data.
At the same time, Beijing ­extended its gold-buying streak for a 13th straight month in November, adding 30,000 ounces to its reserves. That brought its total stock to 74.12 million ounces, worth US$310.6 billion, official data showed.
Amid the rally, the Hong Kong government is rolling out big plans in 2026 to position the city as a global commodities trading hub.

This included plans to establish a gold central clearing system and a gold industry association, and to deepen cooperation with the Shanghai Gold Exchange to expand its influence in international gold pricing, Secretary for Financial Services and the Treasury Christopher Hui Ching-yu said on Monday.
The government’s newly formed Commodity Strategy Committee held its first meeting on Monday under Financial Secretary Paul Chan Mo-po, as Hong Kong looked to build a commodities ecosystem that would generate new growth beyond traditional finance.
“For investors, the question is not whether gold is ‘too high’, but what role it plays,” said Stephen Innes, a managing partner at SPI Asset Management.
“If gold is being treated as a short-term trade, then timing matters and patience is required,” he said. “But if gold is held as insurance against policy error, currency debasement, or systemic shocks, then price becomes secondary to purpose.”
Innes added: “You don’t buy insurance because it’s cheap; you buy it because the cost of not having it rises quietly until it’s too late. And this is the central bank footprint where buyers have become price-agnostic.”

data.title: Free for all or fees for all? Hong Kong’s legal aid levels the scales
data.description:
“Justice is open to all – like the Ritz” is a quote often attributed to Sir James Mathew, a 19th-century Irish judge.
This is another way of saying that while in theory justice and the courts are open to everyone, in practice only those who can afford to pay the hefty fees can get their day in court.
The adversarial system, which is the model adopted in common law jurisdictions, has proved extremely popular in many countries and regions. In particular, cross-examination, which has sometimes been described as “the greatest legal engine ever invented for the discovery of truth”, has captured the imagination of many and is considered an excellent forensic tool.
So far so good, but what about those who seek legal advice or representation but who do not have deep pockets?
In addition to the pro bono schemes run by the Hong Kong Bar Association and other non-governmental organisations, there is legal aid, which has a relatively long history in the city.
The Legal Aid Ordinance was passed in 1966, with the legal aid scheme coming into operation the following year, administered by what was then a sub-department of the judiciary called the Legal Aid Section.
In 1970, the Legal Aid Department was established and moved to the old French Mission Building on Battery Path, which for many years after 1997 was the home of our Court of Final Appeal.
The basic concept, both here and in most other jurisdictions, is that there is both a means test and a merits test.
In the next half-century, the scope of legal aid’s coverage progressively expanded in relation to both civil and criminal matters.
In 1982, legal aid was extended to include appeals to the then apex court, the Privy Council. In 1984, the Supplementary Legal Aid Scheme came into effect for individuals who had a reasonable case but did not qualify for legal aid because they did not pass the means test but were nevertheless unable to pay the private costs of litigation.
It is important, of course, that the level of remuneration provided for legally aided lawyers be set at a reasonable level. In some overseas jurisdictions, because of budgetary constraints, the level became such that it is becoming less and less feasible for barristers to be able to practise in certain areas which are primarily legally aided. This in turn affects the availability of legal representation for those who need it.

It was recently reported that criminal barristers in Northern Ireland are planning to withdraw from Crown Court cases as a result of a dispute about legal aid fees. This is a challenge which is not unique to that jurisdiction.
While there is, of course, always room for improvement and appropriate adjustments, many have acknowledged that the situation in Hong Kong is comparatively better in this respect.
It would be fair to note that legal aid is not without its fair share of attention, in the same way some judgments of the court are. From time to time it will be the case that litigants who have been granted legal aid are pursuing proceedings seeking redress which may not be to the liking of everyone.
It is important to remember that legal aid operates having regard to the means and the legal merits alone. It is not there to judge the litigants or their character.
In an adversarial legal system, it is to the advantage of our community that the courts have the assistance they require to best develop our jurisprudence.
It was recently announced that Chan Chak-ming, a solicitor who served as president of the Law Society of Hong Kong with great distinction, will be taking over as the 10th director of legal aid since the department’s founding.
The department plays a very important part in the administration of justice and also in access to the courts by members of the public. One wishes him well in leading the department, which will continue to open the sometimes heavy doors of justice to all.
Jose-Antonio Maurellet SC is the current chairman of the Hong Kong Bar Association and a commercial litigator.
Legal Tales is a new weekly online column by senior members of the Hong Kong Bar Association presenting their perspectives on current affairs.

data.title: China mall installs glass windows on men’s toilet doors that turn transparent when users smoke
data.description:
A shopping centre in southern China has installed special glass on the doors of men’s toilet cubicles that becomes transparent when people smoke inside, winning applause online.
The move involving bathrooms at Shenzhen’s Shuibei International Centre and Shuibei Jinzuo Building, two jewellery shopping malls in Guangdong province, trended on social media on December 16.

The glass uses a special technology that is normally opaque and cuts off the power in a few seconds after it detects smoke, making the glass transparent.
The shopping centres ensure that bathroom users are aware of the function with a notice on the door that says: “The glass will turn transparent if you smoke. Hold your urge to smoke if you do not want to become famous online.”
Many praised the new technology online. One person said: “Finally, there is an effective measure to tackle smokers.”

Traditional measures such as slogans and small fines have been used in smoking-free facilities but have had minimal effects.
Some also called for the technology to be promoted nationwide.
Shenzhen is a Chinese city that strictly bans smoking in indoor public places. The shopping centres are also non-smoking buildings.
Some expressed fears that transparent glass may infringe on privacy.

A member of staff at the Jinzuo shopping centre, surnamed Zhao, told Shenzhen TV that they began a test run on the glass windows in August and they have received good feedback from shop owners and shoppers.
Previously, the shopping centres have received many complaints about smoking in the bathrooms from customers.
To prevent possible privacy infringements, they said they will calibrate the smoke sensor to ensure that it will only be triggered by smoke.
They will also install a reset button near the glass in case the sensor is accidentally triggered.
The cost of the technology was not disclosed.

According to the local media outlet, Yangcheng Evening News, the measure does not break the law but has legal risks.
Lawyer Lu Weiguo at Guangdong Gehou Law Firm said if the glass windows malfunction and violate users’ rights, the shopping centres would be held legally responsible.
“Shenzhen is truly one of the most pioneering cities in China,” said one online observer.
“I suggest that the shopping centres also install showering systems triggered by smoke in cubicles,” said another.
“We really need such technology in toilets across China. Every time I go to public toilets I get choked by smoke,” said a third.

data.title: China team tackles a century-old puzzle in physics: can we travel back in time?
data.description:
Chinese researchers have proposed a simple yet powerful theory to explain one of physics’ oldest puzzles: why time only moves forward and why travelling to the past remains impossible.
Physicist Cai Qingyu and his team at Hainan University in southern China have developed a fresh explanation at the quantum level for why we cannot unscramble an egg or grow younger, even though the laws of physics perfectly allow time to go backwards.
Unlike earlier theories, their framework does not rely on observations, measurements or external disturbances, the scientists explained in a study published this month by the peer-reviewed journal Annals of Physics.
Instead, it shows that time’s arrow can emerge naturally from how quantum parts become interconnected, a process built into the fabric of microscopic physics itself, they reported.

In the late 1800s, Austrian physicist Ludwig Boltzmann proposed that time moves forward because isolated systems tend to evolve from order to disorder – an idea known as entropy – rather than the other way round.
But a paradox remains. The basic equations that govern motion, from Newton’s laws to the Schrödinger equation, are time‑reversible. If you replaced “t” with “-t” and ran the clock backwards, the equations would still make perfect sense.
To resolve this contradiction at the quantum level, many earlier theories focused on the environment.
When a quantum system interacts with the outside world, fragile quantum states can collapse in ways that cannot be reversed. This breakdown, known as decoherence, has been used to explain the appearance of time’s direction.
In their study, the Hainan University researchers took a different approach to the problem, looking inward at what happens within a closed quantum system where nothing enters or leaves.


In a rare theoretical breakthrough, Cai and his team found that once particles interact and become linked through quantum correlations, those links cannot be undone, effectively locking in a forward direction of time.
Senior theoretical physicist and member of the Chinese Academy of Sciences Sun Changpu, who was not involved in the study, praised the work as a significant step forward.
The study offered deep insights into the fundamental connection between quantum and classical physics, “touching on one of the most profound questions in science”, Sun said, in a statement released by Hainan University.
Cai and his team used correlation to measure how much information was being shared between different parts of the closed system as a way to track what was unfolding over time.
The key result of the study was a mathematical no-go theorem proving that once these correlations form, there is no universal operation that can erase them, according to the paper.
Just as entropy increases over time in classical systems, correlation always grows in the quantum world. Crucially, this growth is irreversible, offering a new internal explanation for why time moves forward, it said.
According to the researchers, this insight offers a unifying explanation: growing correlations not only point to time’s direction in the quantum realm, but also help to explain why entropy increases and other phenomena in the classical world.
They noted that the study remained theoretical, relying on idealised models of a perfectly isolated system. In reality, no quantum system is completely sealed off from its environment.
And while the study connects growing quantum correlations to familiar macroscopic effects, the full bridge between quantum and classical irreversibility has yet to be built. Future work should test these ideas in more realistic settings, the authors said.
Passionate about science? Dive deeper with the Dark Matters newsletter, a weekly in-depth analysis on China’s rise in science, technology and military that goes beneath the surface. Sign up for free now.

data.title: Guest house rates hit 5-year high as tourists flock to Hong Kong for Christmas
data.description:
Tourists planning to spend Christmas in Hong Kong have fuelled a surge in guest house bookings since October, driving occupancy and room rates to their strongest levels in five years.
But those arriving without reservations may end up paying double the price, according to an industry leader.
David Leung Tai-wai also told the Post that the government’s push to promote the “mega-event” economy risked wasting public funds if officials failed to equally promote affordable lodging.
The founder and chairman of the Hong Kong Guest Houses Association – which represents about 750 of the city’s 1,350 budget lodgings – said this year “marked the highest in at least five years”, referring to room rates, occupancy and year-on-year growth.
Occupancy rates at guest houses over the festive period are projected to reach 90 per cent compared with 80 per cent last year, while prices in popular tourist districts have risen by at least HK$50 (US$6.40) to as much as HK$500 a night.
“Last year, people only started calling to book in November, but this year they began as early as October – a full month earlier,” Leung said.
He cautioned that visitors seeking rooms on arrival during the holiday peak could face exorbitant charges as operators capitalised on last-minute demand.
“If you only book on the day, you are definitely being foolish,” he said. “One could end up paying more than double the usual price. It will be miserable.”

Provisional data released by the Hong Kong Tourism Board on Monday showed November visitor arrivals rose by 17 per cent year on year to around 4.2 million.
This was the best performance since 2020 because the mega-event economy helped a full recovery following social unrest in 2019 and the pandemic years, Leung said.
Single rooms for Christmas Eve and Day were priced at HK$380, up from HK$330 last year, while charges for double rooms rose by HK$50 to HK$500, with international tourists accounting for about 70 per cent of bookings.
Visitors from mainland China accounted for about 30 per cent, with the remainder from the rest of the world, including Asia, Europe and the Americas, Leung said.
The total number of visitor arrivals from January to November reached about 45 million, a 12 per cent increase from the same period last year and already surpassing the annual total for 2024.
Leung expects business during Lunar New Year to rise by about 5 per cent – not as strong as Christmas, as people tend to return to their hometowns for family reunions.
He said misconceptions about high accommodation costs were deterring tourists from visiting Hong Kong, arguing that the government’s focus on promoting luxury hotels had left the city’s vibrant budget sector overlooked.
“You can spend billions on mega-events … [but if] nine out of 10 potential visitors think the city is too expensive, they simply won’t come,” Leung said.
He argued that the city’s image as a tourist destination was being skewed by the exorbitant rates of five-star hotels, which often charge between HK$3,000 and HK$4,000 per night, while budget guest houses offering rooms for as little as HK$300 remained largely unknown to visitors.
He said the government only promoted luxury hotels such as The Peninsula and Four Seasons to the world, “never mentioning that one-star or two-star options exist”.
While luxury hotels accounted for less than 10 per cent of the market, their pricing dominated the city’s image, leading most travellers, who were budget-conscious, to believe they could not afford to visit, he said.
The hotel industry is also enjoying a strong season, according to Jack Cheung Ki-tang, a director of CTS HK Metropark Hotels Management, which runs four properties in the city.
He said the occupancy rate for Christmas had already exceeded 90 per cent, with mainland tourists accounting for two-thirds of those bookings.
Christmas rates this year were 15 per cent higher than normal days, with the average price for a standard room at about HK$1,000, he said.
“Our hotel room rates [this year] were about 10 per cent cheaper than last year, except when Hong Kong hosted the National Games,” Cheung said, adding that the prices were attractive.
Occupancy rates for New Year’s Eve and Day were slightly more than 80 per cent, he said.
Caspar Tsui Ying-wai, executive director of the Federation of Hong Kong Hotel Owners, said strong bookings over the festive season would extend to New Year’s Eve and Day, especially in Yau Tsim Mong district.
“The momentum is strong, and we expect a good performance during the holidays, especially in tourist areas, where we anticipate occupancies of more than 90 per cent,” he said.

data.title: Safety gaps, outdated facilities hobbling Malaysian tourism: minister
data.description:
Traveller safety and outdated facilities remain pressing issues that could undermine Malaysia’s tourism ambitions if left unaddressed, according to Tourism, Arts and Culture Minister Tiong King Sing.
Tiong said many attractions lack basic infrastructure upgrades, particularly in destinations frequented by repeat visitors.
“Some foreign visitors tell us that when they came 10 years ago, the facilities were like this, and 10 years later, nothing has changed,” he added.
While Malaysia has produced promotional videos showcasing its attractions, Tiong said many potential visitors have never seen these destinations due to limited marketing reach.
“Our promotion is still not enough. Agencies tell us the same thing – that the promotional budget is insufficient and that we also need cooperation from other countries,” he added.
“When we go to each state, I tell them they must be aggressive. But after that, the states must continue promoting themselves. You cannot just wait for the federal government to do everything.”
On safety, Tiong raised concerns over recurring incidents in Sabah state, citing about 27 tourist deaths linked to weak safety standards.
“Some boats had no life jackets. Some divers had no licences but were still allowed to dive. These are serious issues,” he said, adding that the problems often stem from tour guides and agencies renting out licences.
He said he has raised the matter with Sabah’s new tourism minister and stressed the need for stricter enforcement.
“We must clean this up. Tourists must feel safe and happy so they leave with good memories of Malaysia,” he said.
This story was first published by The Star

data.title: Indonesian fishermen’s climate lawsuit in Switzerland against cement giant to proceed
data.description:
A decision by a court in Switzerland this week to hear a climate lawsuit brought by four Indonesian fishermen against cement giant Holcim has been welcomed by campaigners as an important step for climate justice, even as they warned the legal fight could still stretch on for years.
On Monday, the Cantonal Court of Zug ruled that it would admit the lawsuit, allowing the case to proceed to a full assessment on its merits.
The complaint accuses Holcim, a Swiss company based in the town of Zug, of having “significantly contributed to climate change with its massive CO2 emissions – and continues to do so”, according to Swiss Church Aid, which is supporting the legal action.
Swiss Church Aid HEKS/EPER is a Swiss development and humanitarian organisation founded by Protestant churches that works on social justice, human rights and climate issues. It is backing the plaintiffs in partnership with the European Centre for Constitutional and Human Rights and the Indonesian environmental group Walhi.
The ruling marked the first time a Swiss court had agreed to hear a climate lawsuit against a corporation, Swiss Church Aid said.
“The legal relevance of climate change is now recognised by the world’s highest courts as well as courts in numerous other countries. The development is clear: major greenhouse gas emitters are increasingly being held accountable,” the organisation wrote, adding that it was an “important step” towards climate justice.

The lawsuit was filed in 2022 by four residents on low-lying Pari Island, about a two-hour boat ride from Indonesia’s capital, Jakarta.
According to a statement by the plaintiffs’ lawyers in 2022, while Holcim had not carried out activities in Pari, it accounted for 0.4 per cent of global carbon emissions over the last 100 years.
Holcim had argued that decisions regarding climate protection should be debated in the political arena, not in court, and that “the plaintiffs’ island was doomed anyway”, with or without trial, according to Swiss Church Aid. The cement maker also argued that if it were to reduce its own emissions, other cement and concrete producers “might generate more emissions”.
In response, the court said that “harmful behaviour [is] not legitimate because others behave in the same way”.
Holcim can file an appeal against the decision to the High Court of the Canton of Zug.
Cement production contributes about 7 per cent of global CO2 emissions, according to the Global Cement and Concrete Association.

‘Dire situation’
In Indonesia, Boy Jerry Even Sembiring, executive director at Walhi, hailed the ruling on Tuesday, saying it “comes amid the dire situation of ecological and climate disasters affecting Sumatra”.
He said in a statement: “We have a new precedent for holding large corporations, major emitters, accountable for the climate crisis that has caused these disasters.”
On Pari Island, Edy Mulyono, one of the plaintiffs, said that he was “happy” about the court’s decision because “our struggle so far has not been in vain”.
Edy said that the 43-hectare island, part of Jakarta’s Thousand Islands Regency, had often been inundated by tidal floods, as seawater continued to encroach on the residential areas and groundwater.
“This month alone, there have been six tidal floods. It’s been very bad this month. The food stalls on Perawan Beach, Bintang Beach, have been raised. I thought that after being raised, they would not be submerged in the flood, but they were still submerged. The tidal floods can even be thigh-deep,” the 40-year-old fisherman told This Week in Asia.
According to Edy, the increased frequency and severity of tidal floods have forced his neighbours to raise the foundations of their houses by up to a metre. While the seawater had not reached his home, located about 50 metres (164 feet) from the shore, Edy said that it affected his life in several ways.

At least two times a week, Edy would buy 19-litre bottled water containers costing 27,000 rupiah (US$1.60) each for drinking, cooking and other purposes, except for showering.
“The well water is now brackish. I’m used to bathing with brackish water, but when guests or relatives come from the city, they’re surprised, ‘Why is the water sticky?’ But what can I do? Bathing with bottled water? Where will I get the money to buy it?” Edy said.
Many residents’ electronic appliances had also been broken as they were exposed to the water during floods, he added.
Climate change has also affected Edy’s earnings. Referring to the regular unpredictable weather, he said: “Last week, we couldn’t go out to sea because of bad weather. In the end, we didn’t get [money] because we didn’t go to fish.”
Edy runs a tour boat and a two-room homestay. He said many homestays in Pari Island were empty during the year-end holiday period.
“The Meteorology Agency issued a warning that severe weather and tidal flooding would occur from December 20 to 26, impacting tourist visits, not only to Pari Island but also to other islands in the Thousand Islands [regency].”

Flood protection measures
The plaintiffs filing the climate litigation against Holcim are demanding that the company help residents by providing flood protection measures, such as marine gabions and mangroves, to protect coastlines from abrasion caused by ocean waves, according to Edy.
They are also asking the company to compensate residents for wells contaminated by seawater and provide equipment for processing the brackish well water into water that is suitable for use.
Edy said that it was likely that the plaintiffs would have to wait for a long time for the verdict of the case. “Holcim is a big company, they have a lot of money, they don’t want to lose, and they can appeal.”
Under the lawsuit, Holcim will be asked to reduce its greenhouse gas emissions by 43 per cent in 2030 and 69 per cent by 2040.
According to a Reuters report on Monday, Holcim said it was fully committed to reaching net-zero emissions by 2050 and following a rigorous, science-based approach to achieve the goal. The company added it had reduced direct CO2 emissions from its operations by more than 50 per cent since 2015.
